title: They Made $0 for 4 Years. Then Built a $22B Startup | The Kalshi Story
summary: A video documentary about Kalshi, a regulated financial exchange for real-world events founded by Tarek Mansour and Luana Lopes Lara. The startup spent four years fighting regulatory battles before achieving product-market fit and reaching a $22 billion valuation, demonstrating the challenges of building fintech infrastructure in regulated markets.
writeup: This video traces the founding of Kalshi, the regulated US prediction market, from the core thesis (financial incentives produce more accurate forecasts than opinion alone) through the regulatory battle that nearly killed the company, to the Series F announcement of a $1 billion raise at a $22 billion valuation. The through-line is a company that had to sue its own regulator to exist, won, and then scaled 100x overnight. Founder backgrounds (professional ballet, growing up in Lebanon, Goldman Sachs and Five Rings internships) supply the origin story for both the idea and the discipline that carried it through years of legal deadlock.
key_points: Kalshi's core thesis is that financial stakes eliminate cheap talk: when people put money behind a belief they factor in counterarguments they would otherwise dismiss, which is why prediction market prices outperform polling on events like elections.

The idea crystallized during a 2016 Goldman Sachs internship when investors wanted direct exposure to binary political outcomes (Brexit, the 2016 US election) but had no clean instrument; the only available hedge, shorting the S&P, lost money even when the prediction was correct.

After consulting 60 to 65 lawyers in a single day and receiving uniform rejections, the founders analyzed the 23 core regulatory principles for a licensed US exchange and submitted a detailed compliance framework to their attorney by Monday, signaling seriousness that opened the door to a multi-year regulatory engagement.

Facing a regulator that would not grant approval, Kalshi sued its own regulator, won, and acquired over 2 million customers and $2 billion in trading volume in approximately two weeks with a team of only 20 to 25 people.
In June 2025, Kalshi announced a $1 billion Series F at a $22 billion valuation, doubling the $11 billion valuation it had reached just five months earlier after a $1 billion Series E.

One co-founder trained as a professional ballet dancer in Brazil, sleeping 4 hours a night for 3 years while attending technical school full-time, a discipline she credits directly with the delayed-gratification and goal-focused mindset required to survive a years-long regulatory-first startup path.
